perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the jurisdiction in which the Disclosing Party is located (or if the Disclosing Party is based in more than one country, the country in which its headquart ers are located) (the "Territory") and the parties agree to submit disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement to the non -exclusive of the courts in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the jurisdiction in which the Disclosing Party is located (or if the Disclosing Party is based in more than one country, the country in which its headquart ers are located) (the "Territory").
perturbation.explanation: Removed the clause specifying dispute resolution within the Disclosing Party's jurisdiction and non-exclusive courts. This clause is critical for determining where legal action should be taken and its removal undermines the clarity of legal recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the venue clause, the agreement becomes vague about where disputes should be settled. This ambiguity can create conflict with 28 U.S. Code § 1391, which outlines the rules for venue in federal courts. Without a clear agreement on venue, parties may end up litigating the location of the litigation itself, causing delays and increased costs.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the clause specifying dispute resolution within the Disclosing Party's jurisdiction. This removal contradicts the principle outlined in 28 U.S. Code § 1391, which addresses venue in federal courts. While the statute doesn't mandate a venue clause in every contract, its existence clarifies where disputes should be settled. Removing the clause introduces ambiguity that the law attempts to avoid by providing default rules, as stated in snippet 2: "(a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States". Without a designated venue, parties may face disputes over the location of litigation, potentially leading to delays and increased costs, which the original clause aimed to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon the written request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copi es or extracts thereof, save that where such Proprietary Information is a form incapable of return or has been copied or transcribed into another document, it shall be destroyed or erased, as appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: Immediately upon the written request by the Disclosing Party only after a period of 3 years following termination of the agreement, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof, save that where such Proprietary Information is a form incapable of return or has been copied or transcribed into another document, it shall be destroyed or erased, as appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Modified the clause regarding the return of proprietary information. By adding a delay and restriction on when the information must be returned, it weakens the protection of proprietary information and potentially allows for misuse during the 3-year period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Misappropriation of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying the return of proprietary information could increase the risk of trade secret misappropriation. 18 U.S. Code § 1836 addresses the protection of trade secrets, and hindering the prompt return of such information could be interpreted as creating an environment where misappropriation is more likely, especially if the Receiving Party uses the information in the interim.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 3-year delay before the Receiving Party is obligated to return Proprietary Information, potentially increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. The Law Explanation suggests this delay could be problematic under 18 U.S. Code § 1836, which focuses on the protection of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to violations of trade secret protections, stating, "An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection." The delay weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to promptly reclaim and protect their trade secrets, creating a contradiction by potentially facilitating misappropriation during the 3-year period.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the Parties relating to the subject matter hereof unless any representation or warranty made about this Agreement was made fraudulently and, save as may be expressly referred to or referenced herein, supersedes all prior representations, writings, negotiations or understandings with respect hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the Parties relating to the subject matter hereof unless any representation or warranty made about this Agreement was made fraudulently.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that states the agreement supersedes all prior agreements. The 'entire agreement' or 'integration clause' is crucial in preventing disputes based on prior negotiations, implying that prior agreements could still be valid
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: The absence of a clause superseding prior agreements throws into doubt the integrity of the existing agreement, creating a loophole. If the UCC is applicable, then this would directly be impacted by UCC § 2-202. Without a proper integration clause, the parol evidence rule (UCC § 2-202) may not be properly invoked, making the agreement potentially weaker and allows prior dealings or agreements to influence the enforcement of the written contract. Therefore this omission would contradict the goals of the UCC.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes an integration clause, stating that the agreement supersedes prior agreements. This is a standard clause intended to provide certainty and prevent disputes based on prior negotiations. The changed text removes this clause, creating a loophole that prior agreements could still be valid and influence the enforcement of the current contract. The Perturbation Explanation states that removing this clause implies that prior agreements could still be valid. The Law Explanation refers to UCC § 2-202 and how the absence of this integration clause weakens the agreement. While Scraped Snippets are minimal, the Perturbation and Law explanations clarify the importance of this clause. Removing it creates a Structural Flaw, a Legal Contradiction, by impacting the enforceability of the contract as it relates to prior agreements.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Proprietary Information or (ii) requires the Disclosing Party to proceed with any transaction or relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removed the part which clarifies that the Disclosing Party is not required to proceed with any transaction or relationship. This can generate implied obligation to continue a business relationship, potentially exposing the Disclosing Party to damages if they decide not to proceed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205. Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the clarification that the Disclosing Party is not obligated to continue a transaction or relationship, the agreement might imply a duty to proceed, opening the door to a claim that the Disclosing Party breached the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205. This duty requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of the contract, and suggesting an obligation where none was intended could lead to legal disputes.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a clause that explicitly stated the Disclosing Party had no obligation to proceed with a transaction or relationship. This omission, as explained, could lead to an implied duty to proceed, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While the snippets are not directly relevant, the explanation provided and the modification of the original text suggest a structural flaw that could create a legal contradiction, as the removal of the clause now opens the Disclosing Party up to implied obligation that they originally protected themselves from contractually. This falls under the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction', because the removal of clarifying language impacts the enforceability of the contract, making the modified text open to legal challenges.
